_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Beaver County, PA. Formed 1800 from Allegheny and Washington counties. County seat at Beaver. Ohio River county with early river commerce, the Harmony Society at Economy (now Ambridge), and later steel and manufacturing development. Good record survival from county formation.

previous_courthouses.location: Beaver, PA — near the Ohio River
previous_courthouses.fate: Log courthouse. Beaver County was carved from Allegheny and Washington counties along the Ohio River. The early courthouse served a small frontier population.
Brick courthouse. Served through the canal era, the Civil War, and the early industrial period. Replaced by the current building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Beaver County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or flood. Records run continuously from the county's 1800 formation. Pre-1800 records for the Beaver area are filed under Allegheny and Washington counties. The Harmony Society's meticulously maintained records at Economy (now Ambridge) provide an additional documentary source for the county's early period.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Beaver County after 1800, the courthouse records survive intact. For the pre-1800 period, Allegheny and Washington county records cover the area.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1800 deeds filed under Allegheny and Washington counties.
deeds.repository: Beaver County Recorder of Deeds; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Early estate papers are less complete than bound volumes.
wills_estates.repository: Beaver County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages through church records.
marriages.gaps: 85-year gap between county formation (1800) and marriage license law (1885) filled by church records.
marriages.repository: Beaver County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.repository: Beaver County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.description: The Harmony Society, a German communal religious society led by George Rapp, established Economy (now Ambridge) in Beaver County in 1824. The Society maintained meticulous records of membership, property, and daily operations until its dissolution in 1905. These records document a unique communal experiment and the broader German settlement in the Ohio River valley.
substitute_sources.repository: Old Economy Village, Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission; Pennsylvania State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for tracing German immigrants connected to the Harmony Society. Membership records list names, dates, and origins. The Society's industrial and commercial records also document non-member employees and trading partners in the Beaver County area.
substitute_sources.source: Arndt, Karl J.R. George Rapp's Harmony Society, 1785–1847. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1965.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800–1940. Beaver County is enumerated from the 1800 census. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Manufacturing schedules document river commerce, early mills, and the Harmony Society's industrial enterprises.

substitute_sources.description: Beaver County's Ohio River towns (Ambridge, Aliquippa, Rochester) became industrial centers in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. American Bridge Company (Ambridge) and Jones & Laughlin (Aliquippa) maintained employment records.
substitute_sources.repository: University of Pittsburgh Archives of Industrial Society; Beaver County Historical Research and Landmarks Foundation
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Employment records for immigrant steelworkers and fabricators. Aliquippa in particular was a company town controlled by J&L Steel, and company records — where they survive — document the workforce.
substitute_sources.source: Dickerson, Dennis C. Out of the Crucible: Black Steelworkers in Western Pennsylvania, 1875–1980. Albany: SUNY Press, 1986.

narrative_hooks: Beaver County's records run from 1800, and if {ancestor_name} lived along the Ohio River after that date, the deeds, wills, and court records are intact — for earlier transactions, check Allegheny and Washington county records.

If {ancestor_name} had any connection to the Harmony Society at Economy, the Society's meticulous records at Old Economy Village may hold more detail than the county courthouse — the German communalists documented everything.

Aliquippa was J&L Steel's company town — if {ancestor_name} worked and lived there, the company records may document {pronoun_possessive} employment, housing, and daily life in ways the county records do not.

Pennsylvania's marriage license law began in 1885 — if {ancestor_name} married in Beaver County before that date, the Presbyterian, Baptist, or Methodist church records along the Ohio River are the primary evidence.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Arndt, Karl J.R. George Rapp's Harmony Society, 1785–1847. Philadelphia: University of Pennsylvania Press, 1965.
Dickerson, Dennis C. Out of the Crucible: Black Steelworkers in Western Pennsylvania, 1875–1980. Albany: SUNY Press, 1986.
